They are of single swivel or double swivel types b) Gaseous/Pneumatic nozzles: Used with mist blowers. The atomization occurs by impact of air blast on the drop of a pesticide falling with gravity. Suitable for ULV spraying.

div.head: c) Centrifugal nozzles:
div.p: The spray fluid is fed to a rotating disc and the centrifugal force disintegrates it into droplets of very fine size. Used in rotatary disc ULV sprayers.

div.head: d) Thermal/ hot tube nozzles:
div.p: A fog composed of very fine droplets can be produced by condensing a pesticide which has been injected into a stream of hot gas to shear the liquid into droplets which are immediately vapourized. These vapours later on condense into very fine droplets which are suspended in air for sometime till they finally settle on a surface. It is suitable for controlling flying insects in enclosed spaces.
x)

Spray lances and cut off devices: The nozzle of a sprayer is usually attached to a brass rod (extension rod, spray lance) of variable designs and lengths. It facilitates spraying the lower sides of leaves. This is a simple pump to which a dust chamber is connected. The pump generates an air blast that is passed on to the dust chamber causing the dust to blow out through a delivery tube. It is suited for smallscale operations like kitchengardens, poultry houses, cattlesheds etc. The machine is also known as foot pump duster or cyano gas pump since it can be employed in fumigating the rodent (rat) burrows with calcium cyanide (cyanogas) or sodium cyanide (cynamag).

div.head: Classification of pesticide application equipments
div.p: (II) Bellows duster: It works on compressing bellows (which may be of leather, rubber or plastic) to produce an air blast for ejecting the dust out in small clouds. The machine comes in many shapes and models, and is suitable for domestic purposes or for kitchen gardens.

(III) Rotary (crank) duster: In this machine, the air blast is produced by employing a blower (fan) enclosed in a box. Through gears, the fan is connected to a handle. The slow rotation of the crank is converted into fast rotation of the fan by the gears. The air blast produced by the fan sucks the dust through a suction pipe so as to pass it out through the discharge pipe. An agitator connected to the gears keeps the dust fluffy in the hopper to prevent blocking of the passage to the suction pipe.

Rotary dusters are useful for treating small acreage and row crops like potato, tobacco, cotton etc. Two models of this duster are available one which rests on the belly of the operator and the other mounted on his shoulder.
(IV) Traction dusters: For a large scale dusting, the duster is mounted on wheels and the blower fan is connected to the wheel or wheels. Traction of the duster runs the fan which blows the dust out.

div.head: Poweroperated dusters
div.p: These dusters are provided with a petroldriven motor which runs both the agitator inside the hopper and blower (fan). The blower produces the air blast which, as usual, carries the dust particles to the delivery tube (hose) to be expelled out through the nozzles mounted on a boom. These appliances are used for much larger areas.

div.head: II) Sprayers
div.p: A. Hand operated hydraulic sprayers I) Syringes: Simplest hydraulic sprayers consisting of a cylinder and a plunger. The liquid is drawn in by the upward (suction) stroke and passed out by the downward (compression) stroke of the plunger. These sprayers are tiring to use. Suitable for small areas like household flower beds and kitchen gardens.
II) Bucket (or stirrup) pump sprayers: These machines are composed of a plunger with a D or T shaped handle, one or two barrels (cylinder), a stirrup and a footrest.
i) Single barrel pump Spray is emitted only during downward stroke and intermittently.

ii) Double barrel pump Spray is emitted in both downward and upward strokes because of the provision of a second barrel (broader) which acts as a pressure or air chamber maintaining the spray pressure even during the upward stroke. Continuous spray is emitted.

There are two valves in the doublebarrel pump a suction valve with a strainer at the bottom of the pump and a delivery valve at the bottom of the pressure chamber. These valves allow the flow in one direction only. The delivery tube is fitted on the top of the pressure chamber extending inside it. The pump is dipped in a bucket containing the spray liquid and retained in position by pressing the footrest. Two persons are needed to work on this pump one to operate the pump and other to handle the delivery tube and the spray lance.

div.head: III) Knapsack sprayers:
div.p: This type of sprayer works on the same principle as the stirrup pump but is provided with a tank that sits comfortably on the back of the operator. Accordingly, it is single or double barreled and can be operated by a single person. Suitable for hilly and muddy terrains where use of larger sprayers or power sprayers is not possible.

div.head: IV) Foot (or pedal) pump sprayers
div.p: The piston of this type of sprayer is operated by foot and, therefore the pump has been inverted to connect it to the pedal. Another change is that the pump itself has two sections the lower section containing the piston is the pump proper and the upper section functions as a pressure or air chamber. The liquid is sucked in by the pump through a strainerfitted suction hose and passed on to the air chamber, which, in turn, discharge it through the delivery tube. The pump can develop a pressure upto 200 lbs which can send the spray to the height of 20 ft. Therefore, the pump is suitable for spraying trees in the orchards but can also be used for crop on mediumsized farms.

div.head: V) Rocker sprayer
div.p: Here the piston is connected to a lever (instead of a pedal) which can be rocked to and fro to operate the piston. It has a larger and detachable air chamber.

div.head: VI) Wheelbarrow sprayers
div.p: A rocker or a pedal spray pump can be mounted on a trolley provided with a tank to make a wheel barrow sprayers. The advantage of this machine is that the operator is saved from carrying the weight of the machine and spray fluid and the disadvantage is that it is tiring to push the machine, particularly on uneven grounds.

div.head: B. Compression sprayers I) Pneumatic hand sprayers:
div.p: These sprayers employ air pressure to force the liquid out and, therefore, are kept partly empty to provide space for development of air pressure. They comprise a tank and an air pump to develop pressure and are provided with an aircheck valve at one end and a delivery tube connected outside to a nozzle through a triggercontrol valve.

The sprayer is suitable for the household and for kitchen gardens. Due to the prior development of air pressure, pumping process during spraying is minimized and its negative point is that one has to bend over low plants which is tiring.

div.head: II) Pneumatic Knapsack sprayers:
div.p: These are composed of a strongly built brass or copper tank with a capacity of 725 litres, a vertical air pump passing through the top and tightly fixed to it, a pressure gauge to indicate pressure inside and a delivery tube with a spray lance. The tank is filled upto 2/3 rd to 3/4 th , so as to leave an air space. The pump can produce a pressure of 55 60 lbs/inch 2 . The pump may then be loaded and strapped on the back and the trigger valve of the lance pressed to release the spray. The sprayer is good for large areas and is particularly good for public health insects.

div.head: C. Air Blast Sprayers
div.p: Hand Atomizer (or Flit pump): This is the simplest air blast sprayer which consists of a pump, bearing a minute hole called nozzle in the middle of its closed end, a small tank, a cap and a small delivery tube that rises above the cap upto lowerhalf of the nozzle hole and below it upto the bottom of the tank. The barrel bears a small hole at its posterior end for the incoming and outgoing air. When the plunger is pushed forward, the pump ejects a gust of air from its nozzle which in turn drives the air above the delivery tube away creating a momentary vacuum due to which the spray fluid rises out of the tube. Now, when the plunger is drawn backward (upstroke), fresh air enters the barrel through its nozzle only to be expelled out by the next downstroke. The outgoing (second) gust of air not only breaks (atomizes) the oozed out spray liquid on top of the delivery tube into minute droplets but also carries them along to the target. A succession of up and down strokes thus throws out intermittent jets of the spray liquid. Working the pump is a tiring process, and therefore, it is suitable for household and kitchen gardens.

div.head: D. Poweroperated sprayers
div.p: I) Hydraulic (Power) sprayers: These machines are powered by 15 hp petrol engines and are composed of a pump, a tank with an agitator, a pressure regulator valve, a pressure gauge, suction and delivery hoses(13) having a spray gun or lance with a nozzle or several nozzles mounted on a boom.

According to their capacity to discharge liquids, they can be categorized as low pressure (15 125 psi), low volume sprayers (5100 litres/min. delivery) or high pressure (4001000 psi), high volume sprayers (40450 lit/min.delivery). High volume spraying needs the water and obviously more labour, and is suitable for partially soluble or low dispersibility chemicals of highly soluble products. Due to high degree of dilution, the degree of phytotoxicity is minimized and clogging is avoided due to larger apertures of the nozzles.

div.head: II) Spray blowers:
div.p: These combine a low pressure low volume hydraulic sprayer and an air blower. Atomization of the spray fluid is first done by pump by forcing the liquid to pass through a series of nozzles.

div.head: E. Miscellaneous appliances q
div.p: Mist blowers, Fog generators, Smoke generators, Soil injecting guns q Liquefied gas aerosols, Dust applicators for burrows.

div.head: F) Electrostatic sprayers
div.p: Electrostatic sprayers have been used to serve the agricultural, sanitization and industrial markets. The sprayers can effectively apply most commonly used chemicals, pesticides, disinfectants and additives; providing with 2550% better coverage than a conventional sprayer or fogger. These are of various types but two are given here as under: i) Backpack (BP) electrostatic sprayer q Includes 1 MaxCharge™ airassisted electrostatic spraygun, 2 or 3headed sprayguns available q Backpack available in 9.5 and 15.1 litres sizes q Standard 150 ft hose, 250 ft optional q Requires an external air supply q PVC tank with UV inhibitors q Padded nylon straps q Air line pressure 60 70 PSI q Spray range of 15 20 ft (4.6 to 6.1 m) q Compatible with all conventional, waterbased chemicals and fungicides ii) Mobile backpack (MBP) electrostatic sprayer q Includes 1 Max Charge™ airassisted electrostatic spraygun, 2headed sprayguns available q Ergonomically balanced cart is easy to maneuver; handles provided front and back for 2man team operation q Sturdy construction-built to withstand hard use q Backpack available in 9.5 and 15.1 litre sizes q Standard 100 ft hose, 250 ft optional q 6.5 HP Briggs and Stratton gasoline engine provides power for onboard compressor q Compatible with all conventional, waterbased chemicals and fungicides Two things are important with electro static sprayers: Keeping the nozzles clean and monitoring the electronics. With the small orifices it is imperative to thoroughly flush the units after each use and then to check the spray patterns of each nozzle.

div.head: Maintenance and repair
div.p: Pesticides are very expensive, so must be applied efficiently. The proper maintenance of equipment is, therefore, essential, yet users seldom pay much attention to routine cleaning and checking of equipments. Careless handling and poor maintenance can ruin even the best equipment. Years of good performance can be obtained when the machines are used and serviced correctly.

The proper operation, maintenance, and repair of pesticide application equipments require some considerations before using, while using and after using the equipments. a) Before using the equipments i) Before putting into service, the recommendations for lubrication, operation and maintenance should be carefully read.
ii) The lubrication instructions should be followed faithfully using the specified lubricant.
iii) It should be given a careful general inspection for cracks, loose connections or other malfunctions that may have resulted from vibrations in earlier usage.
iv) In order to apply the correct dose of the chemical per hectare, the machine should be calibrated.

v) Clean water should be used for preparing spray fluids which should anyway be strained. It is important to mix wettable powders with a small amount of water to form a paste before adding the remaining water to it which avoids the formation of lumps. In the case of motorized knapsack sprayerscumdusters, the spray fluid should be filled into the tank, using a funnel. The fluid must not fall on the carburetor and the spark plug.
vi) Only those formulations should be applied with a machine for which it is designed.
vii) The spray fluid in the tank should be agitated to mix it well. After every interruption in the spraying operations, the spray fluid should be again agitated.
viii) In the case of difficulty in fitting a plastic hose or parts, it is advisable to dip them into hot water for a short time before fitting. This will prevent the cracking of these parts.
b) While using the equipments i) Nozzlesetting is important with hydraulic sprayers, because this influences the spray pattern.
The height of the nozzle should be adjusted to suit the crop and wind conditions.
ii) Nozzles should be examined for any deformity or inconsistency in the spray pattern, as also for their blockage.

iii) During the spraying operation, close watch should be kept on the pressuregauge. This is necessary to ensure that the required pressure is being maintained. iv) When changing from one duster to another, the machine should be cleaned out and the application rate rechecked. The same setting will not necessarily give the same rate with a new material.
v) When changing from one chemical to another, it may be essential to thoroughly clean the spray system to remove the chemical used earlier.
vi) Hoses should not be bent at angles because bending results in reduced pressure.
vii) Engines should not be run continuously for long periods because they get overheated.
viii) The operator should walk at a constant speed for ensuring a uniform distribution of the material.
A slight alteration especially in the case of concentrate sprays, may lead to uneven coverage that may give discouraging results in pest control.

div.head: c) After using the equipments
div.p: Pesticide application equipment, like other machines, need periodic repair and replacement of parts.
The maintenance after pesticide application falls into following categories: a) There is the daily routine of cleaning, adjustment, greasing and lubrication.
b) At the end of the season, all wearing parts, such as nozzles, pumps, valves, nuts and ball bearings, should be inspected.

c) A similar examination should also be made of hoses and plastic parts which crack or break due to prolonged use and ageing. d) In order to prevent untimely breakdowns and delays in the middle of the season of heavy demand, it is desirable to arrange necessary replacements well before the start of the season and also have some extra parts.

div.head: Defects common to several types of manually operated sprayers and their remedies
Common mistakes during application of pesticides and their corrective measures
div.p: The proper way to use pesticides involves many safety precautions and it is quite easy to forget some of them. Infact, the person who follows them all is rare, while the person who misses out on some is frequently seen. Some commonly seen mistakes which occur during field application of pesticides are listed below along with the necessary corrective measures:

div.head: Common mistakes
div.p: Corrective action q Sprayer leaking on the back of operator q Spillage of spray solution on sprayer body at the time of pouring q No washing of sprayer buckets, measuring jar, etc. after the operation q Worn out nozzle q Smelling of pesticide q Opening of pesticide bottle with bare hands q Mixing pesticide with bare hands q Smoking, chewing tobacco, drinking water or eating during spraying q Use of unwashed protective clothings q Spraying on an empty stomach q Children, cattle and pets present at spraying site q Child labour as spray operator q Spraying with cuts and open wounds on hands, face and feet q Washing of sprayer, buckets, measuring jar in river, stream or pond q Empty containers are used for storing food or drinking water Clean the nozzle with the help of a thin grass stem or petiole of the leaf or with some needle, if available q q q q q q q q q q q q q q q q Spillage soaked in dry soil and buried away from water source, use hand gloves Not correct. Read label and leaflet carefully before use and observe strictly the safety precautions Dangerous practice. Spray during cooler hours of the day. It is for one's benefit. Red is most toxic product followed by yellow, blue and green in descending order.
q Spillage soaked in a duster with bare hands or washed away with water q Label and leaflet not read before use q Spraying during hotter hours of the day q Unaware of toxicity symbols q q q q

figure.head: Fig. 10
figure.figDesc: Fig. 10 Perennial pest n. Severe pest: They have EIL below the GEP. Regular and constant interventions with insecticides are required to produce marketable crops. EIL decreases as the value of crop increases. It also depends on the stage of the crop, stage of the pest etc., e.g. yellow stem borer of rice, Scirpophaga incertulus.Causes for insects attaining pest status q

: a) ECs are easy to handle, transport and store b) Little agitation required; will not settle out or separate when equipment is running c) Not abrasive d) Will not plug screens or nozzles e) Little visible residue on treated surfaces Limitations: a) Some may be phytotoxic b) In some cases compatibility can be a problem (fertilizers) c) High a.i. concentration makes it easy to overdose or under dose through mixing or calibration errors d) Easily absorbed through skin of humans or animals e) Solvents may cause rubber or plastic hoses, gaskets, and pump parts and surfaces to deteriorate f) May cause pitting or discoloration of painted finishes g) Flammable, should be used and stored away from heat or open flame h) May be corrosive

: a) Relatively easy to handle, transport and store b) Remain in solution; little agitation required c) Not abrasive to equipment d) Will not plug screens and nozzles e) Leave little visible residue on treated surfaces f) Less loss through evaporation Limitations: a) Difficult to keep pesticide on target-high drift hazard b) Specialized equipment required c) Easily absorbed through skin of humans or animals d) Solvents may cause rubber or plastic hoses, gaskets, and pump parts and surfaces to deteriorate e) Calibration and application must be done very carefully because of the high concentration of active ingredient

: a) Usually ready to use formulations b) They are effective where moisture from a spray might cause damage c) They require simple equipments d) They can be used in water scarcity situations e) It is cheaper pesticide formulation f) It can be applied very fast Limitations: a) Easily drift off target during application b) They may irritate eyes, nose, throat and skin c) They are difficult to get an uniform distribution of particles on surfaces d) Their residues easily moved off the target by air movement or water e) The cost of carriers used in the preparation of this formulation is comparatively more f) Because of drift problem there is less deposition of the pesticide on treated plant surfaces and hence effectiveness is reduced g) Will not stick to surfaces as well as liquids h) Dampness can cause clogging and lumping

i) Stability in storage and absence of caking ii) Quick formation of suspension and slow settling out of solid particles iii) Good wettability and spreading on treated surface and iv) Retention on treated surface for a longer period Merits: a) Easy to store, transport, handle, less skin and eye absorption b) Less harm to treated plants, animals and surfaces Limitations: a) They often clog nozzles and pumps, abrasive to pumps b) Inhalation hazard to applicator while measuring and mixing the concentrated powder c) Require good and constant agitation (usually mechanical) in the spray tank d) Difficult to mix in very hard, alkaline water e) Residues may be visible on treated surfaces

g. Aluminium phosphide, EDB, EDCT, MB b) Toxic to a wide range of pests c) Can penetrate cracks, crevices, wood, and tightly packed areas such as soil or stored grains d) Single treatment usually kills most pests in treated area Limitations: a) Fumigants are limited to live plants or products in tight containers b) The target site must be enclosed or covered to prevent the gas from escaping c) Nonspecific, they are highly toxic to humans and all other living organisms d) Require the use of specialized protective equipment, including respirators specifically approved for use with fumigants.

figure.head: Fig
figure.figDesc: Fig 11. A typical Pesticide Product label
of volume of spray fluid per unit area, spraying is classified as:

figure.head: q
figure.figDesc: The rate of application of spray fluid per unit area is determined by four factors: a) Operating pressure b) Size of nozzle orifice c) Swath width d) Speed of the operator Swath width: It is the width of the area sprayed during a spray in one direction. It is space covered effectively by the pattern of the nozzle.

figure.head: i
figure.figDesc: ) Container: A small sprayer is mostly provided with a builtin container. A good container has number of qualities: (a) The size of container should be such as to provide for about 15 minutes of spraying. (b) The tank should be of non corrosive material such as plastic brass, steel or glass (c) the tank should have rounded corners and bottom to facilitate cleaning (d) the lids and caps to all openings should be leak proof. (e) The container should have strainer in filler hole (f) It should also be possible to drain and clean the container quickly.

All carbamates are derivatives of carbamic acid. Many of the carbamic esters are insecticidal and contact poison Dimilin 25 WP 4640

that acts by inhibiting chitin synthesis, thus interferes a few are effective molluscicides. Like organophosphates, the carbamate insecticides interfere in cholinergic transmission. Carbamates have an analogous action, carbamylating rather than with formation of cuticle

figure.figDesc: It has marked translaminar and systemic activity. It shows a strong repellent antifeedant action on both lepidopterous larvae and mites. It appears to interfere with amine mediated control of nervous and endocrine systems in a variety of ways. It has good ovicidal activity. Non toxic to nontarget organisms except predaceous mites. It is a nonsystemic insecticide and acaricide with contact and respiratory action. It is used to control red spider mites, leaf miners, scale insects, and aphids. Amitraz has been found to have an insect repellent effect, works as an insecticide and also as a pesticide synergist. Less harmful to mammals.
figure.note: : Pyrroles are oxidative phosphorylation inhibitors. It works by uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation from electron transport process in mitochondria. Oxidative phosphorylation is the process through which ATP is synthesized in plants and animals. It interferes with formation of ATP which is essential for muscle contraction. Example: Chlorfenapyr: It is an acaricide and insecticide. Chlorfenapyr has broad spectrum of activity against many species of Coleoptera, Lepidoptera, Acarina and Thysanoptera. It is mainly a stomach poison and has contact action also. LD 50 value: 626 mg/kg. Formulation: 3 SC (Pirate, Pylon). K) Formamidines: Formamidines are represented by Chlordimeform and Amitraz with very unique actions by acting as agonists of octapamine receptors. Octapamine act as neurotransmitter, neuromodulator and is involved in energy metabolism and stress responses. Examples: 1. Chlordimeform: LD 50 value: 523 800 mg/kg Formulations: 50 SP, 20 EC Trade names: Acarac, Amitraz, Baam L) Ketoenols:

figure.figDesc: It is active by contact to mite eggs, all nymphal stages and adult females. It is selective, nonsystemic foliar insecticide and acaricide. It is effective against mites and San Jose scale. Spirotetramat is effective against aphids, whiteflies, scales, mealybugs, psylla, phylloxera, thrips, and mites on crops like citrus, vegetables, grapes, potato, other tuberous crops, livestock commodities, and greenhouses/nurseries. It is a systemic insecticide which is sprayed on the plants.
figure.note: M) Diamides: These include1. Chlorantraniliprole:Chlorantraniliprole is a novel anthranilic diamide insecticide, efficacious for control of lepidopteran insect pests as well as some species in the orders Coleoptera, Diptera and Hemiptera. It is active on chewing pest insects primarily by ingestion and secondarily by contact. It exhibits larvicidal activity as an orally ingested toxicant by targeting and disrupting the Ca 2+ balance. It has low mammalian toxicity. Chlorantraniliprole can be used as foliar spray on insect pests of fruits and vegetable crops @ 10 to 60 g/ha. Trade names: Coragen 20 SC and Altacor 35 WG.

figure.figDesc: Blowing the nozzle with mouth Repair the leakage Use a funnel Wash sprayer, buckets, measuring jar, etc. after the operation. Change nozzle cone or disc. If not repairable, replace it Dangerous practice. Do not smell the pesticide. Use hand gloves and apron Dangerous practice. Mix pesticide with the help of a stick/rod Dangerous. Wash hands and face with soap and water before smoking, chewing tobacco and drinking water or eating Dangerous activity, source of contamination. Use washed protective clothings every day Not good. Have breakfast or a full meal before the start of the spray Not advisable. Keep away children, cattle and pests from the spraying site Not advisable. Employ male adult labour as spray operator Seal the cuts and open wounds with medicated water proof tape before the start of the spray Dangerous act. Never do it. Wash them away from the water source Dangerous practice. Rinse them with water thoroughly. Break them and bury them away from water source Dangerous practice.

div.head: SAFE PESTICIDE HANDLING
div.p: Danger of exposure always exists whenever pesticides are handled. The greatest risk to the applicator is in handling and applying toxic materials and in mixing and loading pesticide concentration. Before beginning pesticide handling activities, be sure and prepare to deal with emergencies such as spills, injuries and poisoning. Emergencies supplies should include atleast; q Personal decontamination equipment and supplies: Clean water, detergent and paper towels in a protected container to allow for fast decontamination in an emergency q First aid equipments q Spill cleanup equipments Remember that pesticides are poisonous and they are to be handled with utmost care. Keep the following things in mind while dealing with the pesticides: A) Purchase q Buy the correct products. Buy only recommended products in required pack for use. Check carefully the brand as well as chemical names q Do not buy damaged packs. Check seals carefully for signs of tampering. Refuse to take any pack that is damaged, leaking or whose seals have been tampered with or which do not possess original labels q Purchase the pesticides from authorized dealer or distributor. This ensures to get a genuine product q Check the date of manufacture and expiry on the pesticide pack. Do not purchase dateexpired stock q Insist on getting a bill q Do not purchase any loose pesticide

div.head: B) Transport
div.p: q Obey laws and regulations regarding transportation of pesticides q Keep pesticides away from passengers, livestock and food stuffs q Load and unload carefully q Transport pesticides alone from retailer shop to the farm house

div.head: C) Storage
div.p: q Do not store pesticide along with food or animal feeds q Keep pesticides under lock and key away from children and animals q Keep in a cool dry place. Check labels for advice on storage. Keep away from fires and direct sunlight q Never store pesticides for farm use in living quarters q Inspect package regularly for leaks and signs of damage q Do not store pesticides in a damp room q Do not store pesticides on the floor. Instead store them in a rack q Do not store pesticides in refilled packs. Store in original packs q Do not store empty containers on the floor but break them and keep in a drum till their destruction q Do not store pesticides along with seeds q Do not eat, drink, smoke or chew tobacco in the pesticide store q Do not have temporary and old electric wiring connection in pesticide store

div.head: D) Measuring and mixing of pesticides q
div.p: Always adhere to the recommended doses and dilutions q Read the label and instructions leaflet carefully to determine the proper dose rate, mixing instructions and equipments to be used q Ready for use solid/liquid products such as dusts, granules and ULV sprays can be tipped or scooped from their packs directly into application equipment q Do not use bare hands while handling pesticides q The tank should be filled with water to the correct level and mixed with a stick q Do not fill sprayers upto the brim, they may spill during use q If a stock or spray liquid is being prepared, do not make up more than what can be used up during the same day. Avoid keeping spray liquid overnight q Wear protective clothing while spraying q Do not measure out or mix pesticides in or near houses or where livestock are kept q Take care not to contaminate water supplies or puddles from which animals may drink q Use suitable equipments for measuring pesticides q

Use buckets or open topped drum and use stick or paddle for mixing q Never suck liquid pesticide with mouth, even with a tube q Handle dusts and wettable powders carefully to avoid fluffing up q Wash all equipments after use q Mixing vessels and measures used for pesticides must not be used for any other purpose q Closeup packages after use to prevent leakage or contamination and store safely q Small quantities of leftover and unwanted concentrates should be tipped into a hole in the ground away from dwellings, wells, water ways and crops E) Application equipment -Use, maintenance and repair q Clean and check equipment at the end of each day's operations. Pay particular attention to thorough cleaning of the equipment especially if not to be used again for sometime because residual pesticides may cause corrosion and clogging q Take the most needed spare parts and tools into the field, so that running repairs can be
